module_map.responsibility: Central request/response dispatch: loads middleware chain, routes sync/async calls via BaseHandler, wraps views in atomic transactions
HTTP primitives: HttpRequest (with query params, POST data, files, cookies, session) and HttpResponse hierarchy including streaming and file responses
URL routing: pattern matching, resolver registry, named URL reversing, path converters
ORM layer: model definition, QuerySet API, SQL compiler, connection/transaction management, migrations
Request/response processing pipeline hooks: CSRF protection, caching, security headers, common redirects, locale, gzip, CSP
View callables and class-based views: generic CRUD, list/detail, date-based, error handlers, debug views
Template engine: parsing, rendering, context, loaders, template response
Settings management via lazy proxy (LazySettings), global defaults, project template
Application registry: discovers installed apps, models, and signals at startup
Signal/event bus: weak-reference-based pub/sub for decoupled intra-framework communication
Shared utilities: encoding, functional helpers, deprecation, datastructures, logging, async compatibility, decorators
Form and field validation, widget rendering, model forms

pipeline_trace.description: Server calls WSGIHandler.__call__ or ASGIHandler.__call__. The environ dict is converted into an HttpRequest object. The handler invokes its middleware chain built by BaseHandler.load_middleware.

Each middleware's process_request (sync) or __call__ (async via MiddlewareMixin) runs in order. SecurityMiddleware enforces HTTPS/headers; CsrfViewMiddleware checks token; CommonMiddleware may redirect. Any middleware may return an HttpResponse early, short-circuiting the chain.

BaseHandler.resolve_request calls get_resolver(urlconf).resolve(path). URLResolver walks registered URLPattern/URLResolver tree, matches path, instantiates converters, returns ResolverMatch with (view_callable, args, kwargs). Attaches resolver_match to request.

BaseHandler._get_response (or _get_response_async) calls resolver_match.func(request, *args, **kwargs). Class-based views route to method handler (get/post). make_view_atomic wraps view in a DB transaction if needed. process_view middleware hooks fire here.

View calls Manager/QuerySet methods (filter, get, all). QuerySet is lazy; evaluation triggers SQLCompiler to generate and execute SQL via the database backend connection. Results are hydrated into Model instances.

View (or TemplateResponse) calls Engine.get_template then Template.render(context). Loaders find the template file; the Node tree is evaluated with a RequestContext that includes context processors (auth, request, csrf). Produces a SafeString.
process_response hooks run in reverse order. CacheMiddleware may store the response. SecurityMiddleware adds headers. GZipMiddleware may compress. Response is fully populated.

WSGIHandler converts HttpResponse to WSGI iterable (status, headers, body). ASGIHandler sends http.response.start and http.response.body ASGI events. request_finished signal fires after response is sent.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Nearly every module imports `from django.conf import settings` for configuration values. It is a global read-only dependency with no upstream module dependencies itself, making it the single widest fan-out hub in the framework.

Provides foundational primitives (LazyObject, cached_property, SafeString, MiddlewareMixin, encoding, functional helpers) consumed by nearly all other packages. Has minimal inbound dependencies, making it the core utility layer everything else is built on.

Defines the shared exception hierarchy (ImproperlyConfigured, ValidationError, PermissionDenied, Http404, ObjectDoesNotExist, etc.) used across db, forms, middleware, views, urls, and template. Cross-cutting exception types create implicit coupling to this module.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Async-compatible structured logging middleware that captures per-request metadata (matched view name, ORM query count, total response time) and emits a single structured log entry (JSON-friendly) after each response.
new_types: StructuredLoggingMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin): async-safe middleware with __call__ and __acall__ that records start time on request and emits structured log on response
RequestLogRecord(TypedDict): captures view_name (str), method (str), path (str), status_code (int), duration_ms (float), query_count (int), user (str|None)
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Follow django/middleware/common.py: subclass MiddlewareMixin from django.utils.deprecation; implement both process_request and process_response for sync, and override __acall__ for async (same pattern used by CsrfViewMiddleware and SecurityMiddleware). Use django.utils.log logger naming conventions (django.request). Instrument query count via django.db.connection.queries_log length delta (already used by django/test/utils.py CaptureQueriesContext).
change_proposal.integration_point: Add 'django.middleware.logging.StructuredLoggingMiddleware' near the top of settings.MIDDLEWARE (after SecurityMiddleware, before CommonMiddleware) so it wraps the full view/ORM lifecycle. It stores request start time in request._log_start and query count snapshot in request._log_query_start; process_response computes deltas and calls logger.info with a structured dict.
risks: Query count instrumentation via connection.queries requires DEBUG=True or explicit queries logging enabled; in production with DEBUG=False, connection.queries is not populated by default, requiring use of django.db.backends signal (connection.execute_wrapper) instead
MiddlewareMixin async support depends on asgiref sync_to_async wrapping; incorrect __acall__ implementation can silently fall back to sync execution, breaking async performance characteristics
Logging structured dicts (not strings) may break existing logging handlers/formatters configured by projects that expect string messages; needs a dedicated logger name to allow opt-in filtering
Middleware position affects query count accuracy: placing after session/auth middleware includes their queries; placing before inflates duration with middleware overhead not attributable to the view
Adding timing to every request introduces a small but non-zero overhead (time.monotonic call, dict allocation) that accumulates under high concurrency
